We are experiencing this issue as well, in the wild.  We are seeing Crashlytics reports from users.  Some devices we've seen from Crashlytics include:

* vivo S1 Android 8.1.0
* LGE Nexus 5X  Android 8.1.0
* Motorola One Vision Android 8.1.0
* OnePlus8Pro Android 11
* OnePlus8Pro Android 8.1.0
* Huawei Enjoy 9s Android 8.1.0
* AOSP on IA Emulator Android 7.0
* Xiaomi Mi 9X Android 8.1.0
* AOSP on IA x86_64 Emulator Android 8.1.0

However, I have not been able to reproduce it locally in the emulator on a real device by testing it.

We are building appbundles for our application, so it builds shared objects for the following architectures:

* `arm64-v8a`
* `armeabi-v7a`
* `x86_64`

Question: can an `x86` device run one of the aforementioned builds (in emulation mode)?  Are flutter apps expected to work at all on `x86` type devices?

One issue with Crashlytics reports is that I can't tell what the CPU architecture is from the device that's reporting the crash.  I also cannot tell if the crash is coming from an emulator or a real device.

> I read this doc: https://docs.flutter.dev/deployment/android#what-are-the-supported-target-architectures
> 
> Flutter does not currently support building for x86
> 
> So, i add this into my product:
> 
> defaultConfig { ...
> 
> ```
>     ndk {
>         abiFilters 'arm64-v8a', 'armeabi-v7a', 'x86_64'
>     }
> }
> ```

Assuming you are building an Android appbundle, this shouldn't be necessary.  Flutter already builds those three architectures.  It doesn't try to build `x86`.
Also, with this filter apk even isn't installed

> > I read this doc: https://docs.flutter.dev/deployment/android#what-are-the-supported-target-architectures > > Flutter does not currently support building for x86 > > So, i add this into my product: > > defaultConfig { ... > > ``` > > ndk { > > abiFilters 'arm64-v8a', 'armeabi-v7a', 'x86_64' > > } > > } > > ``` > > Assuming you are building an Android appbundle, this shouldn't be necessary. Flutter already builds those three architectures. It doesn't try to build `x86`. It's not actually true. If you run **flutter build appbundle** and then unzip generated app-release.aab file. You will notice that it contains the following folders: base/lib/arm64-v8a, armeabi-v7a, mips, mips64, x86, x86_64. At the same time, after manual adding abiFilters in build.gradle, flutter generates only folders with names specified in abiFilters. Besides, you can check it out in Google Console: App Bundle -> *select latest release* -> Native Platforms. You will probably see: For some reason, Flutter sometimes doesn't apply default architecture filter, which allowes to build app bundle only for supported architectures. In my particular case this issue appeared after adding library https://pub.dev/packages/flutter_pdfview.

Has anyone tried load Flutter with this library? https://github.com/KeepSafe/ReLinker 
Right now, I don't have an app published on Play Store to try it, but the idea is this: On our Android app, load "flutter" lib with ReLinker before loading Flutter. Then when Flutter Android SDK loads, when on FlutterJNI class call to the system function that loads Flutter lib, the System will not load again because Relinked registers it before. So, it can be a workaround. If anyone can try it please share the results. 🙏

@mikeesouth the latest version is 3.7.9.. I am facing problem at 3.7.8 and going to give a try to 3.7.9.

Flutter currently does not support AOT compilation on x86 platforms. It only supports JIT compilation on x86, as a result app runs without crashing on the emulator in debug mode, but crashes in release mode (#9253).

Some devices running x86 support armeabi-v7a emulation ([9253#issuecomment-665736044](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/9253#issuecomment-665736044)) so they can still run the app. As a result, I think the solution right now is to use `abiFilters` until Flutter gets x86 support.

```
ndk {
    abiFilters 'arm64-v8a', 'armeabi-v7a', 'x86_64'
}
```

But to avoid confusion, since x86 AOT builds are not supported anyway, they should be disabled by default either from the flutter new project template or implicitly.
